Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New2]] }} [[ja:加牟那塚古墳]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamunazuka Kofun}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{Kofun navbar}}

But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kamunazuka Kofun | image = [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New2]] }} [[ja:加牟那塚古墳]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamunazuka Kofun}}

Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:加牟那塚古墳]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamunazuka Kofun}} {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kamunazuka Kofun | image = [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:加牟那塚古墳]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|Kamunazuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]]

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kamunazuka Kofun | image = [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[ja:加牟那塚古墳]] [[sn:Kamunazuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|Kamunazuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{Kofun The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} {{citation {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kamunazuka Kofun | image = [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|280px]]
Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|1=Flower mirror|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Kamunazuka Kofun}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kamunazuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|1=Flower mirror|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} {{citation The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. Kofun and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka 3-chome | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Diameter: 45m
Height: 7.3m | period = Late 6th century | burial = {{ill|Corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|04.51|N|138|32|37.01|E|map_size:5000_region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kamunazuka Kofun}} | burial_individual = | burial goods = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa|12=draft|12=draft}}, fragments of {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, mirrors, glass beads, etc. | Historic Site = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site | map = {{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kamunazuka
Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=04.51|lon_deg=138|lon_min=32|lon_sec=37.01|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Kamunazuka Kofun''' (加牟那塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Chizuka. It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|1=Flower mirror|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view toward rear wall) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage (view toward entrance) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage (view toward main chamber) File:Kamunazuka Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Kamunazuka Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Kofun == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Yoshio Sakamoto, "Kamunazuka Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 1: Archaeology (Sites)*. * Yoshio Sakamoto, "The Emergence of burial Clusters," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Overview Vol. 1: Ancient and Early Modern History*. * Kazuhiro Hosaka, "Haniwa," *Yamanashi Prefectural Historical Records Vol. 2: Archaeology (burial goods)*. * Manabu Kawanishi, "Clay Analysis of Haniwa from Kamunazuka Kofun." * {{Cite book|author=Fumiya Iida, Tokuichi Sakamoto, Koretsuna Kobayashi|year=1978|title=Meiji, Taisho, Showa: Kofu|series=Hometown Memories Photo Collection Vol. 10|publisher=Kokusho Kankokai}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ubazuka Kofun|ja|姥塚古墳 (笛吹市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Kamunazuka Kofun}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:11]] During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|1=Flower mirror|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods from the kofun are housed in the {{ill|Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Museum|ja|山梨県立考古博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Reference images == File:Kamunazuka Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (view It has been Historic Site1 a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site under the name "Kamunazuka"{{Cite web|title=Yamanashi designation1 Guide (Database) Historic Sites 02|publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture|url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/bunka/maibun/yamanashinobunkazai_shiseki002.html#SHI0019|accessdate=2020-05-11}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated in central Yamanashi, at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft|12=draft}}. It lies on the gentle slopes of the left bank of the {{ill|Arakawa River (Yamanashi)|ja|荒川 (山梨県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the boundary between Chizuka and Yumura districts. The area is part of the region known for its dense distribution of late-period kofun with {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, though many have been lost to urbanization. Nearby burial sites include the {{ill|Manjumori Kofun|ja|万寿森古墳|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Yumurayama Kofun Cluster, as well as the Tengu-sawa Kofun, the largest stone-piled mound in Yamanashi Prefecture. The region’s toponym "Chizuka" reflects this legacy{{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. During the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}}, this area was near the boundary of the {{ill|Koma District|ja|巨摩郡|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi District|ja|山梨郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Archaeological evidence from sites like the Matsunoo Ruins suggests that this region was significant in the spread of Buddhism and political power during the late 6th to early 7th century. == Excavation and burial goods == [[File:An Kamunazuka ancient Kofun in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|240px|Kamunazuka Kofun in the early Taisho period.]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Kamunazuka Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the stone chamber}}]] Kamunazuka Kofun has been known since the Edo period. It is mentioned in the 1601 survey records for Chizuka Village and the comprehensive regional history *{{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft|12=draft}}*, under various names such as "Kanazuka," "Kamazuka," and "Kamizuka." Excavations in 1969, conducted by Professor Shoichiro Yoshida of Aoyama Gakuin University, revealed the tumulus's two-tiered structure, with a diameter of 45 meters and a height of 7.3 meters. The mound was covered with {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft|12=draft}} and decorated with {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}}. The burial facility is a south-facing, single-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber built with large stone materials indicative of the late 6th century. burial goods found include {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, small glass beads, and three mirrors, identified as an {{ill|Inner Arc Mirror|ja|内行花文鏡|1=Flower mirror|12=draft|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Divine Beast Mirror|ja|神獣鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, and a {{ill|Daryu Mirror|ja|鼉龍鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the location of these mirrors is currently unknown. Other finds include swords, shields, and various haniwa types, such as ceremonial implements, horse figurines, and warrior figurines. Soil analysis of the haniwa revealed that most were made from volcanic materials sourced from nearby rivers, while some included granite, likely imported from regions like the Sone Hills. burial goods
